coredata.dc:title: Polymer nanocomposite film for dual colorimetric and fluorescent ascorbic acid detection integrated single-cell bioimaging with droplet microfluidic platform
coredata.dc:creator: Alizadeh, Negar
Ghasemi, Foad
Salimi, Abdollah
Hallaj, Rahman
Fathi, Fardin
Soleimani, Farzad
coredata.dc:description: As one of the most important vitamins, which, participates in various physiological reactions in human body, Ascorbic Acid (AA) detected directly through fluorescent and colorimetric method. Our sensing system was based on the non-oxidation reduction strategy using fluorescent polymer nanocomposite (quantum yield: 26%) as a novel probe. The addition of AA can intensively suppress the fluorescence of the polymer nanocomposite through the static quenching effect (SQE). The linear response range of AA was obtained from 1 to 575 μM with LOD of 250 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Additionally, the color of the nanoprobe was turned from purple to colorless, and a visual colorimetric assay has been also developed for the detection of AA. The absorbance at 604 nm is linearly related to the logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 50–425 μM with LOD of 10 μM. More importantly, we have developed a droplet microfluidic approach for intracellular imaging of AA in living cells. Isolated small microdroplet allows the fast detection and imaging of AA in single cells. The current strategy provides an effective platform for monitoring and imaging biomolecules in living cells and could be extended to more broadly in cell biology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Fluorescent
Colorimetric
Nanoprobe
Microfluidic
Single cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of AA in aqueous solution
Imaging of AA in living cell using the microfluidic device
Detection of AA in cell extracts
Mechanism of AA suppressed fluorescence of PNF probe
Selectivity of nanoprobe with different ions and biological molecules
Fluorescent and colorimetric detection of AA using the PNF
Detection of AA in cell extracts
Imaging of the PNF probe in living cells using droplet microfluidic platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of detecting ascorbic acid in the single cell-encapsulated droplets in combination with PNF nanocomposite.

(A) SEM image of PNF (B) FTIR spectrum of (a) potassium oxalate, (b) PNF and (c) thionine, (C) the PL (smooth line) and UV–vis (dotted lines) spectrum change of PNF probe before (a, c) and after addition of AA (b, d) and (D) PL spectra of diluted aqueous solution of PNF at different excitation wavelengths.
(A) The PL, (B) UV–vis spectrum of the different probe in the presence of AA, (C) FTIR spectrum and, (D) fluorescence lifetime of PNF and PNF + AA.

(A) Time-dependent PL intensity of PLNPs upon addition of AA, and (B) Selectivity of sensing system towards AA and other biological relevant reactive species. Experimental conditions: 500 μM AA, 1 mM of other interference including (a) dopamine (DA), (b) urine acid (UA), (c) glutathione (Reduced) (GSH); (d) glucose, (e) homocysteine, (f) arginine, (g) cysteine, (h) proline, (i) creatinine, (j) serine; (k) alanine, (l) l-therionine, (m) Hg2+, (n) Mg2+, (o) K+, (p) Ca2+, (q) Na+, (r) AA.

(A) Fluorescence and (C) absorbance responses of PNF upon addition of various concentrations of ascorbic (0, 1, 75, 125, 175, 225, 275, 325, 375, 425, 475, 525, 575 μM) and (0, 50, 125, 175, 225, 275, 325, 375, 425 μM), respectively, the linear calibration plot of (B) absorbance and (D) F–F0/F0 versus the concentration of AA. (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of PNF in the absence and presence of ascorbic acid, respectively).
(A) MTT assay of the PNF probe with different concentration and (B) Fluorescence intensity at 630 nm of PNF probe with (a) and, without (b) 20-fold diluted cell extracts.
Fluorescent images of HeLa cell in microdroplet (A) cells incubated without PNF probe, (B) cells incubated with 250 μg mL−1 PNF probe, (C) cells were treated with (100 μM) and (D) (200 μM) AA.
Analytical characteristics of different fluorescence or colorimetric AA measuring system.

head.ce:title: Polymer nanocomposite film for dual colorimetric and fluorescent ascorbic acid detection integrated single-cell bioimaging with droplet microfluidic platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscale Physics Device Lab (NPDL), Department of Physics, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Western Ontario, N6A 5B7, London, Ontario, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the most important vitamins, which, participates in various physiological reactions in human body, Ascorbic Acid (AA) detected directly through fluorescent and colorimetric method. Our sensing system was based on the non-oxidation reduction strategy using fluorescent polymer nanocomposite (quantum yield: 26%) as a novel probe. The addition of AA can intensively suppress the fluorescence of the polymer nanocomposite through the static quenching effect (SQE). The linear response range of AA was obtained from 1 to 575 μM with LOD of 250 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Additionally, the color of the nanoprobe was turned from purple to colorless, and a visual colorimetric assay has been also developed for the detection of AA. The absorbance at 604 nm is linearly related to the logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 50–425 μM with LOD of 10 μM. More importantly, we have developed a droplet microfluidic approach for intracellular imaging of AA in living cells. Isolated small microdroplet allows the fast detection and imaging of AA in single cells. The current strategy provides an effective platform for monitoring and imaging biomolecules in living cells and could be extended to more broadly in cell biology.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), known as vitamin C, is a strong antioxidant and a cofactor in enzymatic reactions, which can prevent free radical induced diseases, especially Parkinson's disease and cancers [1,2]. It is due to that AA can efficiently scavenge toxic free radicals and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) that are associated with several forms of tissue damage and disease. Furthermore, AA is a vital vitamin and natural organic compound in the human diet [3]. It is therefore of main importance to developing facile, rapid and simple analytical methods for highly selective and reliable measurement of AA. In the recent years, various approaches have been developed for the quantitative determination of AA, such as chemiluminescence [4], titration [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], electrochemical [7], colorimetry [8], and fluorescence assays [9]. Among the available techniques to detect and quantify AA, fluorescent methods have attracted extremely interest due to their high sensitivity and selectivity, inexpensiveness, easy operation and nondestructive in live cells or tissues [10–12]. Therefore, to date, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing fluorescent probes for AA such as graphene quantum dots (GQD) [13], persistent luminescence nanoparticles (PLNPs) [14], up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [15], 7-hydroxycoumarin [16], and carbon dots (CDs) [17]. There are two main mechanisms used to design fluorescent probes for AA. One is based on using reducing fluorescence quenchers on the surface of fluorescent probe and turning-on the fluorescence signal [18,19]. For instance, Liu's group developed a highly photoluminescent glycine (GLY) functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GLY-GQDs) for the selective detection of AA after quenching by a certain amount of Ce4+ [20]. Tang's group designed cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH)-modified graphene quantum dots (GQD) for direct bioimaging of endogenous ascorbic acid in living cells [21]. Cobalt oxy-hydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes was also used as a quencher; however, in the presence of AA, the CoOOH was reduced to Co2+ by AA, resulting in fluorescence recovery of the GQD. These methods based on reducibility of AA disturbed by some reducing substances in human serum such as dopamine, uric acid and glutathione. Another mechanism is the non-oxidation reduction strategy based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (SQE) mechanism [22,23]. Rezaei et al. synthesized silver nanoparticles for simple detection of ascorbic acid via IFE [24]. Colloidal silver nanoparticles (SNPs) have been produced by chemical reduction of silver ion in the presence of AA. The absorption band of SNPs entirely covered both emission and excitation bands of CdTe quantum dots. Thus, SNPs act as a strong fluorescence quencher for the QDs. The non-redox mechanism does not require any covalent linking between the receptor and the fluorophore or surface modifications of fluorescence nanomaterial, which can be an effective tool for the construction of simple, sensitive and rapid fluorescence sensors [25,26]. Simplicity and low detection limit of fluorescence analysis offer an opportunity for determination and imaging of AA at the cellular level [27].

Droplet-based microfluidics have been developed for manipulating and processing small (10−6 to 10−15 L) amounts of droplets or plugs carried by their immiscible phase [28,29]. Droplets can encapsulate cells and analysis of droplet-encapsulated single cells is of particular interest for sensitive investigation as molecules released from the cells remain confined in the droplet at a high effective concentration [30,31]. Droplet-based microfluidics were investigated for a wide range of high-throughput single-cell measurements [32,33]. Microfluidics help to improve sensitivity, reduce cost, and minimize operator influence in the operation of biological assays [34]. In 2016, D. A. Mongersun et al. utilized a droplet microfluidic platform for the determination of single-cell lactate release and measure the cell-to-cell variance of lactate release in K562 leukemia and U87 glioblastoma cancer cell lines. In another study in 2018, Shen and co-worker reported sensitive detection method for single-cell secreted H2O2 by integrating a microfluidic droplet sensor with Au nanoclusters [35]. They developed a platform to generate signals dependent on the analyte concentration within ultra-small, volume well-defined liquid droplets.

Based on the abovementioned consideration, we report a novel fluorescent probe for non-redox detection of AA and combined with the use of a droplet microfluidic platform to the screening of AA on living single cells. Polymer nanocomposite film (PNF) was used as a probe, which, synthesized using thionine and potassium oxalate at room temperature under sonication. The fluorescence intensity of PNF can be efficiently quenched by the addition of AA, and also its color turned from purple to colorless (Fig. 1 ). This phenomenon is due to pH change of thionine induced by potassium oxalate and formation of a non-fluorescent complex between the AA and the thionine. Most of the previously reported works were based on the redox reaction mechanism, which cannot effectively avoid the interferences from some reductive biological molecules in vivo like cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH) [36]. However, our non-redox fluorescence strategy based on SQE could effectively avoid the interference of the reducing substances and did not require the complex probe modification. The developed approach provided a wide linear range and a low detection limit for the fluorescence and colorimetric determination of AA. Further, the PNF was evaluated as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the intracellular imaging of AA in single living cells using droplet microfluidic method.

3,7-Diaminophenothiazin-5-ium (thionine), potassium oxalate (K2C2O4), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), glucose, l-glutathione (GSH), KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, alanine (Ala), arginine (Ag), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (H-Cys), tryptophan (Thr), proline (Pro) and serine (Ser) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the reagents were used without any further purification. Deionized water from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore) was used in all solutions and experiment.

The fluorescence spectra and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured at room temperature on a spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer and a SPECTROD 250-analytikjena spectrophotometer (Germany) respectively. The morphology of product was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) on a MIRA3 TESCAN HV: 20.0 KV from Czech Republic and the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a Vector-22 BRUKER spectrophotometer (Switzerland).

The facile procedure of PNF synthesis is described as follows: 2 mg of thionine (TH) was dissolved in 5 ml water. Subsequently, 5 mL aqueous solution of potassium oxalate was added into the above solution. Afterward, the resulted solution was sonicated for 30 min; then, the obtained mixture was centrifuged and washed three times with deionized water. Finally, the obtained product was dispersed in water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The quantum yield (Q) of PNF was determined by using fluorescein in 0.1 M NaOH as the standard sample and calculated according to the following equation:
Q = QR*(Grad/GradR)*(n2/n2 R)
Where Q is the quantum yield, Grad is a gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance, n is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for water). The subscript R refers to the reference with a known quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: 300 μL volume of freshly prepared PNF probe were added to 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes, and then different concentrations (0, 1, 75, 125, 175, 225, 275, 325, 375, 425, 475, 525, and 575 μM) of AA solutions were added. Immediately, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 570 nm. Furthermore, for colorimetric assay, different concentrations (0, 50, 125, 175, 225, 275, 325, 375, and 425 μM) of AA solutions were added to the same solution in the microcentrifuge tubes. The color mixture was changed from purple to colorless. After 4 min of waiting, the mixture can be used for measuring absorption spectra with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The PNF sensing system was tested for its selectivity towards AA. The selectivity study was carried out by adding 10 μL of some interferences including (glucose, GSH, DA, UA, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ala, Ag, Cys, H-Cys, Thr, Pro and Ser) in PNF sensing system. The fluorescence was recorded after addition of interferences, the same as the steps described above.

To test the cytotoxicity of PNF, human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells) were used in this study, and were cultured in cell medium (DMEM), supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, penicillin (100 U mL−1), and streptomycin (100 mg mL−) in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2. HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate in cell medium overnight and then incubated with different amount of PNF (25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 μg mL−1) in cell medium for 2 h. After that, the medium was replaced with DMEM (100 μL per well) containing MTT (0.5 mg mL−1) followed by the incubation for 4 h. The medium was removed and the violet frozen crystals were dissolved with DMSO (100 μL). Finally, the absorbance intensity at 565 nm was recorded by a microplate reader.

Initially, a silicon mould was fabricated by Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) system. A mixture of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) prepolymer and curing agent (10:1) was poured onto the pre-etched silicon mould followed by curing at 80 °C for 12 h. Then, the cured PDMS was peeled off and placed on the clean bare glass substrate. To better adhesion of PDMS channel to the glass, the sample was annealed at 80 °C for 2 h. The inlets and outlets were punched and connected to tubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of AA in living cell using the microfluidic device
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells were trypsinized and counted before being used in microscopy or microfluidic studies. After that, the cells were washed three times with 1 mL PBS buffer and then incubated with the DMEM culture medium containing 250 μg mL−1 nanoprobe at 37 °C in 5% CO2. For cell encapsulation within droplets, aqueous streams of cells and nanoprobe were introduced to horizontal channel and, the oil-phase stream was introduced to vertical channels using peristaltic pumps (ISM834C). The fluorescence changes of nanoprobe uptaked by Hela cell in the microdroplets were monitored through an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus FV1000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in cell extracts
ce:section.ce:para: The Hela cells (1.0 × 106/mL cells) were first centrifuged for 3 min at 25 °C with the supernatant being removed, and the cells precipitate was redispersed in 1 mL PBS buffer. Then, the redispersed cells were subjected to a sonication treatment for 30 min. The crushed cell suspension was centrifuged for 15 min, and the supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further use.

SEM images of PNF are presented in Fig. 2 . A that reveals the size and shape of the nanocomposite. As can be seen, PNF are monodisperse with uniform spherical shape and size around 8 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained to study the composition PNF. The FT-IR spectra of thionine, potassium oxalate and, PNF are presented in Fig. 2. B. The peak at 3410 cm−1 ascribed to the hydroxyl group of carboxylic acid of potassium oxalate. As well as the peaks at 1608 (cm−1), and two closely spaced bands at 1386 and 1316 cm−1 are observed, corresponding to asymmetric ν(C–O) and symmetric ν(C–O) [37], respectively (curve a). Compared to the spectrum of potassium oxalate the intensity and position of the peak at 1608 cm−1 was changed in the IR spectrum of PNF, which can be ascribed to the integration of this peak with C=N bond in the polymer (curve b) [38]. The absorption bands at 3000 cm−1 of monomeric thionine (curve c), attributed to symmetric stretching vibrations of –NH2 and disappeared in PNF which reveals the amine groups have reacted with oxalate [39,40].

Fig. 2. C shows the UV–Vis and the fluorescence emission spectra of PNF in the absence and presence of AA. As exhibited the fluorescence intensity of the PNF decreased significantly after the addition of 200 μM AA, indicating that AA can effectively suppress the fluorescence of PNF (curve c, d). Fig. 2. C also displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of the PNF and corresponding photographs before and after AA addition at room temperature. Before the introduction of AA, the PNF is purple with two absorption band in the wavelength of 284 and 604 nm (curve a). On the contrary, upon AA addition, the resultant PNF turns colorless (inserted images) and both absorbance peak decrease (curve b). Furthermore, the excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of PNF in the range of 500–590 nm is shown in Fig. 2. D. It can be seen that the PL peak approximately stands consistent (at 630 nm) with varying excitation wavelengths and reached to the maximum value at 590 nm, then the PL intensity gradually decreased. Such excitation-independent emission indicates an approximately uniform and well-passivated PNF surface [41]. On the other hand, the enhancement of PL efficiency without a change in its peak position was ascribed to electron filling of nonradiative surface empty states according to the electron filling model [42]. Using fluorescein as a reference, the PL quantum yield (QY) of PNF was calculated, which, estimated to be about 26%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of AA suppressed fluorescence of PNF probe
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL) quenching defined as any process that causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. The forms of PL quenching are divided into two categories, collisional/dynamic quenching and static quenching. In both static and dynamic quenching, the fluorophore and the quencher are contacted [43,44]. In dynamic quenching, the quencher must diffuse to the fluorophore during the lifetime of the excited state. However, in the case of static quenching, a ground-state complex is formed between the fluorophore and the quencher, and this complex is non-fluorescent [45,46]. As shown in Fig. 1, our proposed sensing strategy is based on the (static quenching effect) SQE, where PNF functioned as the fluorometric reporter, and AA served as the fluorescence quencher. This phenomenon may be due to that hydrogen bonds were formed between the hydroxyl groups of AA and the surface functional groups of the PNF. It can be deduced from the FTIR spectrum of PNF after addition of AA, in which the peaks corresponding to ν(C–O) band at 1608 and 1316 cm−1 was decreased or disappeared (Fig. 3 . C). For investigation of quenching sensing mechanism, we compare colorimetric and fluorescence response of thionine and PNF toward AA. As can be seen in Fig. 3. A, B the thionine emitted at 630 nm and exhibits a UV–vis absorption band centered around 604 nm, in the absence of AA. In the presence of 200 μM of AA, no decrease in absorbance and fluorescence intensity was observed. While, after addition of 200 μM AA to the proposed nanocomposite the fluorescence and absorbance intensity of PNF decreased by 65%. By measuring the solution pH, it was found that the pH of PNF decreased compared to pH of thionine. Hence, this quenching can be related to the pH change induced by addition of potassium oxalate to thionine. In order to check such supposition, we synthesized the PNF in the PBS buffer solution and evaluated the response of proposed composite toward AA. Fig. 2. A, B indicate that the addition of AA had no significant effect on the fluorescence and absorbance peak of this composite. Therefore, we can conclude that the interaction of potassium oxalate cause changes the pH of thionine, so that in which pH a non-fluorescent complex is formed between the AA and PNF probe. Although the other compound can be used to change the pH of thionine, we employ this prepared polymer nanocomposite, because fluorescent nanocomposites have the more photostability and biocompatibility compare to dye molecules [47].

Both dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and static quenching effect (SQE) fit into Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = Ksv[Q] + 1, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of AA, KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the AA concentration. Therefore, the quenching mechanism could not be simply differentiated by this equation. In order to study more about the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted. The excited state lifetime for static quenching effect remained constant; but, in dynamic quenching suppression, the ratio τ0/τ of the lifetime is proportional to the ratio of F0/F. As presented in Fig. 3. D, the fluorescence lifetime of the PNF probe was obtained as 2.75 ns, and it was about 2.69 ns with the addition of AA. The fluorescence lifetime of PNF shows no significant change in the absence and presence of AA, indicating that there is no obvious excited-state interaction between PNF and AA [48]. This observation reveals that SQE may be responsible for the fluorescence quenching of PNF caused by the AA. So, with the assistance of the non-redox SQE mechanism, a simple, rapid and selective approach for AA monitoring was developed. This sensing strategy gives new insight into the design of efficient detection of AA due to its remarkable advantages including easy preparation of sensing probe using nonspecific and, special reagent at room temperature, no requirement of complex treatment of the metal nanocomposite and fast responding of prepared sensing probe. Fig. 4 . A presented reaction time of PNF probe with AA. As can be seen, PNF-AA could rapidly reaction completely in 4 min and kept stable during the following 10 min, suggesting a promising application in fast sensing of AA without long time control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of nanoprobe with different ions and biological molecules
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of this sensor towards AA, the response of the probe toward various interferences including cations, small biomolecules and, amino acids were tested under optimized conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4. B, in the absence of any reactants (the control background sample), and other non-target molecules 1 mM of glucose, GSH, DA, UA, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ala, Ag, Cys, H-Cys, Thr, Pro and Ser, the fluorescence intensities were strikingly larger than that of AA target (500 μM), suggesting the no interactions between the interferences and PNF. Hence, this sensing platform based on PNF probe can be applied as a selective nanosensor for AA determination without significant interference from usually common compounds in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric detection of AA using the PNF

ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was exploited to determine the cytotoxicity of PNF probe. We performed the MTT assay in Hela cells with different nanoprobe concentrations (25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 μg mL−1). Then the cell viability was evaluated by the ratio of the absorbance of the cells incubated with the nanoprobe to that of the cells incubated with culture medium only. As shown in Fig. 6 . A, it was found that the cell viability of Hela cells was 87%, with the concentration of the nanoprobe up to 250 μg mL−1. The MTT assay suggested that the nanoprobe shows low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility in living systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in cell extracts
ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring the distribution of reactive species such as AA in living systems have great importance for understanding their physiological functions, pathological effects and also early diagnosis of some serious diseases [55]. To test the feasibility of the designed method, the nanoprobe was then used to detect endogenous AA in cell extracts. As shown in Fig. 6. B, with 20-fold diluted cell extracts was added, the fluorescence intensity at 630 nm of the PNF nanoprobe was decreased. This result corroborated that the PNF probe was feasible to detect AA in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of the PNF probe in living cells using droplet microfluidic platform
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the simplicity of our designed method and very low cytotoxicity of the PNF probe, we established the ability of the probe for bioimaging application. We chose Hela cells as the model system and the results were analyzed in droplet microfluidic platform using an optical microscope. Trapping the cells in microdroplets is an effective analytical tool allowing sensitive measurements on individual cells in minimized reaction volume that are typically not accessible via bulk cellular measurements [56]. The microscopy images showed that the cells without treated with probe displayed no fluorescence emission (Fig. 7 . A). However, when cells incubated with PNF probe, the fluorescence emission could be seen in the red channel (Fig. 7. B). Then, the Hela cells were incubated with different concentration of AA (100 and 200 μM) for intracellular imaging. As a result, with the AA concentration increased, the fluorescence emission of the red channel was much decrease than that without treated with AA (Fig. 7. C, D). Such results demonstrate that the integration of microfluidic approach with PNF probe shows potential for detection and imaging of AA at single-cell level.

In summary, a facile strategy was developed for sensitive detection of AA using PNF probe through a non-redox mechanism. The proposed sensing strategy is one step without any need for modification of the nanoprobe. The mechanism is based on SQE effect, allowing a simpler, time-saving and cost-effective way for AA sensing. We have verified the high sensitivity and selectivity for this new approach through both fluorescence and colorimetric method. The nanoprobe showed a good linear range of response for AA concentration of 1–575 and, 50–425 μM for fluorescence and colorimetric assay, respectively. Then, we have developed a droplet microfluidic for cellular imaging of single cell encapsulated microdroplets. This technique provides a robust method and ideal platform to study single-cell and promising tool for molecular biology assays in clinical assessment.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest to the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Research Office of University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086) and Iranian Nanotechnology Innovation Funds for research on Smartphone and microfluidic-based sensors.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ascorbic acid: much more than just an antioxidant
Biochim Biophys Acta Gen Subj

RSC Adv

J Pharm Biomed Anal
